perturbation.original_text: Upon the request of the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confide ntial Information at Disclosing Party’s request, or at Disclosing Party’s option, certify destruction of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the request of the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information within 180 days of Disclosing Party’s request. Destruction of the same is not allowed.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return deadline to 180 days and prohibiting destruction conflicts with the common law principle of 'reasonable time' for fulfilling contractual obligations, which is generally interpreted to be much shorter, especially given the sensitive nature of confidential information. It also potentially violates trade secret laws by unduly delaying the return of information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted in Massachusetts)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces a conflict because delaying the return of confidential information for 180 days potentially extends the period during which misappropriation could occur, increasing the risk of trade secret violations and undermining the prompt remedial measures expected under trade secret law. Prohibiting destruction also potentially forces the Receiving Party to keep the information longer than needed, thereby increasing the risk of an unintended trade secret leak.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by imposing a 180-day delay on the return of confidential information and prohibiting its destruction. This directly conflicts with the common law principle of 'reasonable time' for fulfilling contractual obligations, particularly concerning sensitive information like trade secrets. The Law Explanation highlights the increased risk of misappropriation and unintended leaks due to this delay. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as an act of acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret without consent, indicating that delaying the return can increase the window for such acts. The original text allowed for immediate return or destruction upon request, but the changed text removes the possibility for immediate action by the Receiving Party, potentially undermining protections against trade secret violations. The prohibition on destruction further exacerbates the risk of unintended leaks by forcing the Receiving Party to retain the information longer than necessary.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confident ial Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive and other equitable relief (without bond and without the necessity of showing actual monetary damages) as may be deemed proper by a court.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled to injunctive relief only if actual monetary damages are proven in court.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring proof of actual monetary damages for injunctive relief contradicts the established legal principle that injunctive relief is available to prevent *threatened* misappropriation of trade secrets, without necessarily requiring proof of existing damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted in Massachusetts)
perturbation.law_explanation: By explicitly requiring proof of actual monetary damages as a prerequisite for injunctive relief, the modified text contradicts the purpose of M.G.L. c. 93, § 42A, which allows for injunctive relief to prevent *threatened* misappropriation of trade secrets. The modification effectively eliminates the ability to prevent future harm, limiting the remedy to situations where damage has already occurred.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42A Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42A Trade secrets; injunctive relief Section 42A Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42A: Trade secrets; injunctive relief [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42A. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined upon principles of equity, including but not limited to consideration of prior party conduct and circumstances of potential use, upon a showing that information qualifying as a trade secret has been or is threatened to be misappropriated. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate any economic advantage that otherwise would be derived from misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the law by requiring proof of actual monetary damages for injunctive relief, while M.G.L. c. 93, § 42A allows for injunctive relief to prevent *threatened* misappropriation of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Section 42A, explicitly states that "(a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined upon principles of equity...upon a showing that information qualifying as a trade secret has been or is threatened to be misappropriated." The changed text removes the ability to prevent future harm by demanding that damage has already occurred.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Massachusetts and both parties further consent to jurisdiction by the state an d federal courts sitting in Boston , Massachusetts .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Delaware, regardless of conflicts of law principles. Both parties consent to binding arbitration in Wilmington, Delaware, waiving their right to a jury trial.
perturbation.explanation: Switching the governing law to Delaware and mandating arbitration with a jury trial waiver can be problematic. While parties can generally choose governing law, Massachusetts has a stronger interest in regulating agreements made within its borders. The jury trial waiver must be knowing and voluntary; burying it in the choice of law could make it unenforceable. Additionally, the agreement does not contain language about how the arbitrator is selected, which could pose enforceability issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the agreement affects consumers in Massachusetts, mandating Delaware law and arbitration could violate M.G.L. c. 93A, especially if the Delaware arbitration process is less favorable to consumers than Massachusetts courts. The change attempts to circumvent consumer protection laws by imposing an out-of-state jurisdiction and dispute resolution process. This is relevant as Data Boiler Technologies LLC is a Massachusetts corporation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Massachusetts to Delaware, coupled with mandatory arbitration and a jury trial waiver, creates a contradiction under M.G.L. c. 93A, particularly if the agreement affects consumers in Massachusetts. The Law Explanation states that mandating Delaware law and arbitration could violate M.G.L. c. 93A if the Delaware arbitration process is less favorable to consumers than Massachusetts courts, particularly since Data Boiler Technologies LLC is a Massachusetts corporation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 outlines the rights of individuals to bring action against unfair trade practices in the superior court, which is circumvented by the mandatory arbitration clause in Delaware. The change attempts to circumvent consumer protection laws by imposing an out-of-state jurisdiction and dispute resolution process. This creates an Inconsistency Legal Contradiction, because it sets a requirement that violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party may assign, delegate or otherwi se transfer this Agreement or any of its rights or obligations hereunder without the other party's prior approval.
perturbation.changed_text: DBT may freely assign this agreement to any affiliate or successor without notice or consent from XXXXX.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing only one party (DBT) to freely assign the agreement without the other party's consent creates an imbalance. This could be deemed unconscionable or violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, particularly if assignment significantly disadvantages XXXXX.
perturbation.law_explanation: Unilateral right to assignment could be construed as an unfair or deceptive practice under M.G.L. c. 93A, especially if XXXXX relies on DBT's specific expertise or financial stability. Allowing assignment without consent could force XXXXX to disclose confidential information to an unsuitable assignee, causing harm and potentially violating the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of fairness and equal rights in contract assignments. The original text requires mutual approval for assignment, ensuring both parties agree. The changed text allows DBT to unilaterally assign the agreement, potentially disadvantaging XXXXX. This contradicts M.G.L. c. 93A, Section 2(a), which declares unlawful 'unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce.' The unilateral assignment, especially if XXXXX relies on DBT's expertise, could be deemed an unfair practice.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include any information that: (i) is or subsequently becomes publicly available without Receiving Party’s breach of any obligation owed to Disclosing Party; (ii) became known to Receiving Party prior to Disclosing Party’s disclosure of such information to Receiving Party; (iii) became known to Receiving Party from a source other than Disclosing Party other than by the breach of an obligation of confidentiali ty owed to Disclosing Party; (iv) is independently developed by Rec eiving Party without access to the Disclosing Party’s information , or (v ) the Confidential Information is required to be disclosed pursuant to a requirement of a governmental agency or law so long as the other party is provided notice of such requirement p rior to any such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include any information that: (i) is or subsequently becomes publicly available; (ii) became known to Receiving Party prior to Disclosing Party’s disclosure of such information to Receiving Party; (iii) became known to Receiving Party from a source other than Disclosing Party; (iv) is independently developed by Receiving Party; or (v) the Confidential Information is required to be disclosed pursuant to a requirement of a governmental agency or law.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the limiting clauses 'without Receiving Party’s breach of any obligation owed to Disclosing Party' in (i) and 'other than by the breach of an obligation of confidentiality owed to Disclosing Party' in (iii) and 'so long as the other party is provided notice of such requirement prior to any such disclosure' in (v) broadens the exceptions significantly. This weakens the protection of confidential information and shifts the burden to the Disclosing Party to prove a breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows disclosure of confidential information without notice, potentially violating fair dealing standards. Failure to provide notice before disclosure could also be seen as an unfair practice, especially if it results in competitive harm to the Disclosing Party, potentially violating M.G.L. c. 93A.

(b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. perturbation.justification: The original text provided some protection to the disclosing party by requiring notice prior to disclosure and limiting exceptions related to public availability and third-party sources by preventing actions that breach confidentiality. The changed text removes these protections, creating a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation indicates that removing the notice requirement can violate fair dealing standards and potentially M.G.L. c. 93A due to unfair practices that may result in competitive harm. Snippet 1, referencing M.G.L. c. 93A Section 2, declares unfair methods of competition and unfair practices in trade or commerce as unlawful. By removing clauses that protect confidential information, the modified text creates a situation where unfair practices are more likely, thus contradicting the law.
